prompt:
Our batch boxes moved to zsh 5.9 as /bin/sh-for-tooling and I'm done fighting getopts. I want the little wrapper that normalizes a batch-job request into a printable 'job card' rewritten the zsh way: one file, `jobcard.zsh`, in the repo root, run as `zsh jobcard.zsh [options] <job> [arg...]`. Parsing must be a single `zparseopts` call with `-D -E`, one array per option using brace specs — the repeatable one accumulates with a `+` spec, e.g. `{t,-tag}+:=tag_opts` (remember zparseopts stores those as alternating name/value elements). No getopts, no hand-rolled while/shift loop for the recognized options.

Options, each with a short and a long spelling:
- `-t TAG` / `--tag TAG` — repeatable; every occurrence is kept, in command-line order, duplicates included.
- `-r N` / `--retries N` — default 2. N must be a non-negative integer; anything else (including a negative) prints `jobcard.zsh: retries must be a non-negative integer, got: <value>` on stderr and exits 65, nothing on stdout.
- `-o FILE` / `--out FILE` — default `out.log`.
- `-q` / `--quiet` — boolean; giving it twice is the same as once.
- `-h` / `--help` — print the help below on stdout, exit 0.

Both value forms work: separated (`-r 5`, `--tag blue`) and attached (`-ofast.log`, `--tag=green`, `--retries=7`). With zparseopts the attached long form keeps the `=` glued to the value, so strip one leading `=` from every option value; real values never start with `=` here. A literal `--` ends option parsing: it is dropped and everything after it is positional even if it looks like an option. Options may appear after positional words too (that's the -E behavior; the leftovers minus recognized options are the positionals). The first positional is the job name, the rest are its args.

After parsing, anything left that still looks like an option (starts with `-` and has at least one more character, before any `--`) is unknown: `jobcard.zsh: unknown option: <word>` on stderr. zparseopts itself fails when a value-taking option sits at the end with no value — don't let its own stderr chatter leak; report `jobcard.zsh: an option is missing its value` instead. No job name at all is `jobcard.zsh: missing job name`. All three of those go to stderr followed by the usage line, exit 64, stdout empty.

Help text, byte-exact (errors print only the first line after their message):

usage: jobcard.zsh [-q] [-o FILE] [-r N] [-t TAG]... <job> [arg...]
  -t, --tag TAG      add a label to the card (repeatable, order kept)
  -r, --retries N    retry budget, a non-negative integer (default 2)
  -o, --out FILE     log destination shown on the card (default out.log)
  -q, --quiet        mark the card quiet
  -h, --help         show this help

On success print the card to stdout, byte-exact, six lines:

job: <job>
args: <args separated by single spaces, or (none) if there are none>
out: <FILE>
retries: <N in plain decimal — 007 prints as 7>
quiet: <yes|no>
tags: <tags joined with commas, or (none)>

Mind the difference between no tags at all — `tags: (none)` — and one empty tag (`-t ''`), which prints `tags: ` with nothing after the space. Tag values are opaque text: spaces and glob characters like `*.log` come through verbatim, never expanded.

`zsh test_zparse.zsh` from the workspace root is the acceptance gate; it must pass unmodified — don't touch the test file.
